data.title: Hong Kong urged to be more proactive in removing barriers with Shenzhen
data.description:
Hong Kong should take a more proactive approach to removing barriers hindering the cross-border flow of people, capital, goods and data with neighbouring Shenzhen, analysts have said, as the new leadership of the mainland China tech hub signals a fresh push for deeper integration.
The calls for action followed the appointment of Jin Lei, an economist and former official in Sichuan province, as Shenzhen’s Communist Party chief last month, succeeding Meng Fanli, who has since been named the governor of Guangdong province.
Jin’s arrival coincides with Beijing’s push to accelerate Hong Kong’s Northern Metropolis megaproject in the country’s 15th five-year plan, which guides development from 2026 to 2030. The Northern Metropolis scheme, first announced in 2021, aims to transform 30,000 hectares (74,132 acres) of land along the border with Shenzhen into a dual engine for economic growth and housing.
Jin was born in the central province of Henan in 1970 and obtained a bachelor’s degree from Wuhan University’s political science department in 1992. He earned a master’s degree in economics from Xiamen University in 2002, according to the Shenzhen-based Securities Times.

In his first month in office, Jin conducted high-profile visits to major joint projects between the two cities, including the Hetao Shenzhen-Hong Kong Science and Technology Innovation Cooperation Zone, a key component of the metropolis.
During his visit, Jin described the planning and development of the Hetao area as a major mission entrusted by Chinese President Xi Jinping. He pledged to align Shenzhen’s prowess in applied research with Hong Kong’s strengths in fundamental innovation.
“We will proactively coordinate development with the Hong Kong Park, promote the smooth and efficient flow of people, logistics, capital and information,” Jin said, positioning Hetao as a primary engine for the Greater Bay Area.
The bay area is Beijing’s initiative to turn Hong Kong, Macau and nine Guangdong cities into an integrated economic hub.
Jin also visited the Qianhai Shenzhen-Hong Kong Modern Service Industry Cooperation Zone, calling for its transformation into a hub for high-quality opening-up and a frontier for deeper Shenzhen-Hong Kong integration.




Lau Siu-kai, a consultant to the semi-official Chinese Association of Hong Kong and Macau Studies think tank, said the city’s progress in removing cross-border hurdles had been insufficient, highlighting major obstacles in legal systems, mainland restrictions on data outflow and professional protectionism.
“We have actually been working on these aspects, but the progress is not fast enough,” Lau said. “We need to identify specific projects between Hong Kong and Shenzhen that can best attract talent, capital and enterprises to collaborate, and then remove barriers in those areas.”
Lau added that while Shenzhen traditionally took the initiative in pushing for deeper integration, its rapid development had reduced its reliance on Hong Kong. He urged Hong Kong to identify its strengths, particularly in supporting Shenzhen’s small and medium-sized enterprises in expanding overseas.
Peng Peng, executive chairman of the Guangdong Society of Reform, a government-affiliated think tank, said that Jin’s visits to Qianhai and the Hetao area indicated that collaboration with Hong Kong would be a central focus for Shenzhen in the coming years.
“[Shenzhen-Hong Kong collaboration] serves as a crucial pillar in the development of the Greater Bay Area,” Peng said, while pointing to some “pain points” to be addressed for deeper integration.
“There is still some way to go to achieve integrated development, such as the [cross-border] flow of people, technology, capital and data.”
Lawmaker Yiu Ming, deputy chairman of the Legislative Council’s subcommittee on the development of the Northern Metropolis, said integration between the two cities should advance progressively, with the megaproject serving as a testing ground.
Yiu also emphasised that the success of the metropolis depends on close cooperation between Hong Kong and Shenzhen, and said he expected Legco to step up exchanges with the city’s neighbour.

data.title: Returning to a fire-ravaged home in Wang Fuk Court: what to prepare
data.description:
About 1,900 families who lived in Hong Kong’s Wang Fuk Court will return in batches to their fire-ravaged homes from April 20.
Nearly five months after the deadly blaze in Tai Po engulfed seven of the estate’s eight buildings, the government arranged for residents to visit the devastated flats to collect their belongings and bid farewell to their homes.
According to Deputy Chief Secretary Warner Cheuk Wing-hing, around 6,000 residents have booked slots to return, while 47 households opted out, with many deciding against the trip after seeing pictures of their ruined flats.
The Post looks at how residents should prepare and considers the emotional challenges they face in making the trip.
1. Can I make repeated visits?
Yes, you can.
Cheuk said on Sunday that residents could “generally” make multiple visits within the three-hour slot assigned.
Police or Civil Aid Service officers will accompany you when entering and exiting the building, according to Cheuk.
2. What should I wear?
You should wear a light-coloured outfit or a reflective vest to ensure others can see you, as the buildings will be very dark without electricity, according to Teddy Ng of Minimal Funeral, a funeral service provider which arranged a workshop for returning residents earlier this week.
You should also wear a helmet to protect yourself from falling debris, waterproof, steel-toed safety shoes that are easy to clean, and anti-slip, puncture-proof gloves.
“If the damage in your flat is extensive and severe, the metal may have melted, and the wood may have been damaged, meaning there will be a lot of splinters and sharp edges,” he says.

3. What should I bring?
Dr Pasu Ng, founder of the funeral service social enterprise, says a multi-tool kit with a hammer, plier, scraper and screwdriver will be useful for dealing with items damaged by heat.
“If things have melted and stuck together, you will need to pry them open, with those tools you could pry, cut or saw them open,” he says. “You may also bring lubricant to help open the doors.”
You will also need hands-free lights that allow you to climb up and down the stairs while holding the rails and your bags.
Headlights are not recommended as they can cause glare and produce disorientating beams when users turn their heads to speak to others.
Ng also recommends using trolleys with triple-star wheels that can be pulled up the stairs instead of big boxes, which will be hard to carry.
He notes that some elderly people may need walking sticks to climb the stairs and advises using ones with a smaller quad base, as those with a large base may slip off the edge of steps.
A first aid kit is also a must, and should include small packs of saline water to wash out dust that enters the eyes.
4. How do I manage emotional triggers?
A counsellor from the social enterprise, Candy Sum, says experiencing feelings of guilt and anxiety when entering the buildings is normal, but there are ways to manage overwhelming emotions, both for yourself and those around you.
“If you see a person around you who has frozen, you can try gently tapping their arm, call them by their full name, and let them smell something strong like medicated oil – that can help bring them back from a breakdown,” she says.
Another technique is to gently hold the person’s hand and guide them through a breathing exercise – inhale for four seconds, hold for two, and exhale for six – and repeat the process three times. You can also ask them to name five items in sight, to bring them back to the present when they are triggered.
When someone encounters a traumatising sight, those around them can use their bodies to block the person’s view and provide a sense of security.

5. How do I support those who can’t be there?
Clinical psychologist Dr Candy Fong of the Hong Kong Psychological Society says it’s important to take care of those who cannot return to their homes, such as children, elderly people and those with chronic illnesses.
She says returning family members can engage them in the preparation process, including them in discussions and allowing them to share their thoughts and feelings.
You can also capture photos or videos of the home for those unable to make the visit, and search for items of sentimental value to help them process the loss and say goodbye.
6. What should I do after leaving the site?
Fong encourages processing your grief using items retrieved from the flats, such as repurposing the belongings of the dead as a way of connecting with those you have lost.
Feeling emotional after bidding a final farewell is completely normal, she says.
However, she recommends seeking professional help if you are still struggling after one month.

data.title: Winner of second Beijing robot half-marathon smashes human world record by 6 minutes
data.description:
The winner of a robot half-marathon held in Beijing on Sunday smashed the human world record by more than six minutes in the latest display of the industry’s rapid development.
The humanoid device, known as Lightning, was developed by the Chinese smartphone and gadget company Honor, which only entered the sector last year.
Its finishing time of 50 minutes 26 seconds was far quicker than the men’s world record of 57:20 held by the Ugandan runner Jacob Kiplimo and around 17 minutes faster than the quickest human in Sunday’s race, a local amateur runner.
Organisers said Sunday’s humanoid robot half-marathon attracted 105 competitors in contrast to the 21 entries for last year’s event.
Under the rules of the competition, different teams can enter the same model using different hardware and algorithms to control the robot. This year, the top three were all Lightning models operated by different teams.
The winning robot was 169cm (5.5ft) high, weighed around 45kg (99lbs) and was designed to adapt to complex terrains while moving at high speeds.
However, not all the robot runners found it easy going. One ran into a nearby vehicle but managed to steady itself before carrying on.
Another, an H1 model from Chinese robotics darling Unitree, collapsed while approaching the finish line and had to be carried off the track.
Even one of the Lightning models ran into difficulties, hitting a barrier after crossing the finishing line.
Sunday’s event was the second running of the Beijing E-Town Half-Marathon and Humanoid Robot Half-Marathon, which pitted the robots against some 12,000 humans racing along a 21km (13-mile) route in the capital’s Daxing district.
This year’s competition divided the humanoid entrants into two groups, those controlled remotely by humans and those that ran autonomously.
Nearly 40 per cent of the robot competitors were autonomous, including the top three, organisers said.
As well as acting as a showcase for the rapid development of the Chinese robotics industry, the event acts as a testing ground for the robots’ batteries, joints, motor systems and software algorithms as they navigate the sharp bends and slopes found along the course.
Long-distance running is a useful testing ground for robotic applications and can help verify how well their motors endure while making repeated cyclic motions, an essential quality for industrial use, according to Liu Xiangquan, a robotics professor at Beijing Information Science and Technology University.

“The event also provides universities with a valuable platform for hands-on engineering practice, helping students develop real-world technical skills and supplying industry with job-ready talent,” he added.
The development of humanoid robots was identified as a key driver of China’s economic growth along with sectors such as artificial intelligence in an AI action plan published last year.
The country dominates the market for humanoid robots, accounting for 80 per cent of the sector worldwide last year, according to market consultancy Counterpoint.
On Saturday, Beijing hosted another robot challenge in which the machines tackled a series of real-world challenges such as crossing narrow bridges, leaping over gaps, climbing steep slopes and navigating obstacles.
The winner of the inaugural Robot Warrior Challenge was the Tien Kung 3.0 designed by X-Humanoid.

data.title: Chinese drug makers take on Western dominance in US$16 billion Parkinson’s race
data.description:
Chinese biomedical companies deploying cutting-edge technologies are emerging as serious competitors to Western dominance in the race to treat Parkinson’s disease, in a market that could reach US$16 billion over the next decade.
The firms are developing a range of approaches – from autologous cell therapies made from a patient’s own cells and ready-made cell products to gene therapies and non-invasive ultrasounds – although many of these candidates are still in early-stage trials.
Many patients with Parkinson’s disease need to take levodopa – a drug first developed in the 1960s – and other medications that alleviate the symptoms but do not halt the disease’s progression, and whose effectiveness diminishes over time, according to the American Parkinson Disease Association and a 2025 study by the Parkinson’s Foundation.
Now Chinese drug makers are making breakthroughs, clearing significant regulatory and clinical hurdles.
The latest example is Shanghai start-up UniXell Biotechnology, which received clinical trial approval in China in 2024 and the US in 2025 for UX‑DA001, an autologous stem cell therapy that uses a patient’s own cells to replace the dopamine‑producing neurons destroyed by Parkinson’s disease.
Founded in 2021, UniXell has raised more than 300 million yuan (US$44 million) from A and A-plus financing rounds that drew support from state-backed funds, venture capital and pharmaceutical investor Tasly Pharmaceutical, the company announced in February.
Taking a different approach is Xellsmart Biopharmaceutical, with offices in Suzhou and Shanghai, which is developing an allogeneic, off-the-shelf cell therapy. These treatments are manufactured in advance from donor cells and can be administered to any patient without the need for a customised preparation.

Its lead candidate, XS-411, an iPSC-derived dopaminergic neural progenitor cell therapy, received clinical trial approval in both China and the US in early 2025, according to the company’s website.
Xellsmart raised 500 million yuan in combined series C and C-plus funding, drawing investors including China Reform Venture Capital Fund and Suzhou Industrial Investment Social Security Fund, the company said in an April 2 statement.
Chongqing Haifu Medical Technology has developed a third-generation ultrasound device that can treat brain diseases – such as Parkinson’s – minimising the risks associated with invasive brain surgery.
The future of treating brain diseases could lie in non-invasive technologies, said Jill Shen, board secretary of Chongqing Haifu Medical Technology. “The brain is very different, once you cut it open, it has a big impact on the entire brain. That is why many neurological diseases currently have no treatment options.”
Haifu Medical said its technology achieves sub-millimetre precision in deep brain regions.
Founded in 1999, the firm was among the companies recruited by Hong Kong’s Office for Attracting Strategic Enterprises and set up an office at Hong Kong Science Park last year.
In gene therapy, Shanghai Vitalgen BioPharma Co and Belief BioMed are running phase one trials in China for novel adeno-associated virus-based treatment to restore dopamine production.
North America dominated the Parkinson’s disease treatment market with a revenue share of 38.03 per cent in 2024, according to Grand View Research.
BlueRock Therapeutics, a US-based cell therapy developer owned by German pharmaceutical giant Bayer, dosed its first patient in a phase 3 trial last year.
More than 10 million people globally suffer from Parkinson’s, a movement disorder that robs patients of basic motor control, leaving many unable to walk unaided, button a shirt or hold a cup without trembling, according to the World Health Organization and the US National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke. In severe cases, the disease prevents people from getting out of a chair without help.
The global market for Parkinson’s disease drugs reached US$7.75 billion last year and is expected to nearly double to US$15.77 billion by 2034, Fortune Business Insights said in a March report.

data.title: Asia’s supply chains could give it edge over US in AI race: Granite Asia’s Foo
data.description:
As the artificial intelligence race moves beyond language models into the physical world, Asia’s manufacturing and supply chain strengths could give it an edge over the US, says Granite Asia’s Jixun Foo.
The veteran venture capitalist said the current wave of AI development, sparked by breakthroughs in foundation models over the past two years, had entered a new phase where physical applications – from robotics to industrial automation – were becoming increasingly important, playing to Asia’s long-standing manufacturing strengths.
“AI is not just about models or applications in software,” said Foo, senior managing partner at Granite Asia, in a recent interview with the South China Morning Post. “If you look at Asia, our advantage is in the supply chain, hardware and engineering capabilities.”
That advantage, he added, stemmed from a deeply integrated industrial base spanning mainland China, Japan, South Korea and Southeast Asia, allowing faster iteration and large-scale deployment of AI-enabled hardware, which could give Asia an edge as the focus of AI shifted from “language models” to “real-world applications”.
The shift was also being reflected in venture capital flows, with investors increasingly focusing on application-layer companies that could generate tangible use cases and revenue, rather than purely model-driven businesses, according to Foo.

“When we look at [opportunities in] AI, we don’t just look at models or applications in isolation – we look at the entire ecosystem,” Foo said. “The key question is what industries it can transform and what kind of value it can unlock.”
Granite Asia, formerly the Asia operations of GGV Capital, manages about US$10 billion in assets and has backed a number of major technology companies including Baidu and Xpeng. Foo, a Singapore-born investor who has spent two decades in the region, is best known for his early bets on Chinese internet and mobility firms.
He cited Manycore Tech, a spatial computing software developer that debuted on Friday in Hong Kong, as an example. Backed by Granite Asia since 2014, Manycore is positioning itself in the emerging field of “spatial intelligence”, where digital models of physical environments can be used to train embodied AI systems.
That shift, he said, would also fundamentally reshape business models, including those built on advertising, as interactions moved from human attention to machine-driven execution.
For Foo, the long-term opportunity lay less in software interfaces and more in how AI interacted with the physical world. “In the past, software provided tools that people used,” he said. “In the AI era, what it delivers is outcomes – it gets the work done.”
Foo also said hardware and infrastructure would play a more central role in the next phase of AI development, particularly in Asia, where supply chains for components such as chips, optical modules and industrial equipment were already concentrated.
“I tend to believe hardware will become more valuable,” he said, pointing to the rapid development of robotics, autonomous driving and AI-powered industrial systems.
At the same time, he expected a wave of new listings for AI-related companies as they sought to capitalise on investor enthusiasm, though he cautioned that not all would prove sustainable.
“Many companies will add ‘AI’ to their story,” he said. “But over time, the market will differentiate between real and fake AI.”

data.title: Government should probe department heads prudently under new system: Regina Ip
data.description:
The Hong Kong government should use its proposed accountability system prudently to investigate department heads and avoid undermining morale among civil servants, a top adviser to the city’s leader has said.
But Regina Ip Lau Suk-yee, convenor of the city’s top decision-making Executive Council, stressed on Sunday that the system would serve as a reminder for leaders to actively spot deficiencies in their departments and “do better in handling residents’ complaints”.
In a televised interview, Ip, formerly the city’s director of immigration, said the government should not launch an investigation just because a problem was persistent, but also take into account how departments responded and whether any probe truly improved administrative efficiency.
“The government will someday trigger the system after its introduction, but I hope it will launch investigations cautiously to avoid dealing a blow to morale among civil servants, as it is difficult to acquire talent for many senior posts now,” she said.
First mentioned in Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu’s 2025 policy address, the new system is designed to enhance governance, drive top officials to improve work processes and boost management efficiency.
Under the proposed mechanism, the city leader, department secretaries or bureau directors can initiate investigations by the Public Service Commission, an independent statutory body that advises on civil service appointments and promotions, if they deem problems in a department to be “widespread, repetitive and systemic” or if there are indications that department heads are personally implicated.
Meanwhile, department heads will be tasked with investigating problems involving working-level officers that are regarded as “of a less serious and isolated nature”.
Ip said investigations under the proposed mechanism were expected to be rarely triggered, and would serve mainly as a “reminder” for department heads to proactively spot systemic problems.
“They have to proactively pay attention to long-standing problems, and in the long run the departments will be better at handling residents’ complaints,” she added, noting that the system should be preventive in nature.
Hong Kong currently runs an accountability system for political appointees, including all government ministers, their deputies and political assistants, as well as the director of the Chief Executive’s Office.
The proposed system is expected to cover around 60 department heads. But bureau permanent secretaries, who are also civil servants, will be excluded, except those for education and housing.
Echoing earlier comments by Secretary for the Civil Service Ingrid Yeung Ho Poi-yan, Ip said the arrangement was reasonable, as permanent secretaries were mainly responsible for policy formulation rather than day-to-day management of departments.
She added that permanent secretaries currently reported to bureau ministers every day, and a new system was not required to hold them accountable.
“It does not take an investigation to know that a permanent secretary is not performing their duties well,” she said. “But a bureau may oversee dozens of departments, and the secretary may not be able to know the details of their operations.”

Yeung said on Saturday that investigations into problems under the proposed new system would cover anyone involved, including permanent secretaries, to determine where responsibility lay.
The proposed legislation for the new accountability system, including conferring investigative powers on the Public Service Commission, will be submitted to the Legislative Council for “negative vetting” in June, and will come into effect by mid-October if the scrutiny period is extended.
Ip on Sunday also said the government would be introducing a leadership training programme for department heads in mid-2026.
The programme was a response to the increasingly complex governance landscape in Hong Kong, rather than incompetence of civil servants, she explained.
“The smaller picture in Hong Kong now also relies on the country’s broader picture, while the country should also factor in global geopolitics, which are volatile. Many departments are facing new issues,” she said, citing examples such as the cross-border low-altitude economy and the challenges of the Southbound Travel Scheme for Guangdong Vehicles.
“Civil servants need to learn how to harness new scenarios.”
On Sunday, Yeung announced that she was leading a group of more than 20 department heads and permanent secretaries to attend a study session at the National Academy of Governance in Beijing.
The trip would help the group better understand China’s major policies and development strategies, inspiring them to contribute more effectively to formulating the Hong Kong’s first five-year plan, she said.
The Civil Service Bureau and the academy entered into an agreement to provide a dedicated governance programme for the city’s senior civil servants in 2023, with the first study trip under the progamme taking place that year.
